main_summary.summary: Justice Ernest Finney Jr., civil rights attorney and the first African American Chief Justice of the South Carolina Supreme Court since Reconstruction, sits down with interviewer Joe Mosnier in Columbia, South Carolina on June 8, 2011, for the Civil Rights History Project. Finney recounts his birth in Smithfield, Virginia, his upbringing by his teacher father and aunts after his mother's death when he was ten days old, and the family's relocation through Washington, Baltimore, and ultimately to Orangeburg, South Carolina, where his father became Dean of Claflin College in the mid-1940s. He describes working as a soda jerk for the Weber family near Claflin and South Carolina State, witnessing a Ku Klux Klan parade outside the campus, attending South Carolina State University School of Law, and beginning a legal career in 1954 weeks after Brown v. Board. Finney details representing the Friendship Nine sit-in defendants from Friendship Junior College in Rock Hill, his collaboration with Matthew J. Perry Jr. and I. DeQuincey Newman, his work on the South Carolina Advisory Committee to the US Commission on Civil Rights, his resignation following the Orangeburg Massacre, three failed political races before his 1972 election to the SC House, founding the Legislative Black Caucus, election to the State Supreme Court in 1985, becoming Chief Justice in 1994, and authoring the Abbeville County School District v. State of South Carolina education-funding decision.
key_themes: Black legal practice in segregated South Carolina
Orangeburg Massacre aftermath and federal civil rights response
Education-funding equity and the Corridor of Shame
main_summary.historical_significance:
Finney's testimony documents the institutional career arc of a Black civil rights attorney who moved from defending direct-action protesters at the start of the 1960s to occupying the highest judicial office in South Carolina by 1994. His account preserves first-person recollections of representing the Friendship Nine, the disappointment of the federal government's refusal to reinvestigate the Orangeburg Massacre, and his role on the South Carolina Advisory Committee to the US Commission on Civil Rights during a period when the White Citizens' Council was headquartered in the state.

The interview also captures the institutional history of South Carolina State University School of Law, which Finney graduated from in 1954 and which the state later closed — a closure he contrasts with the continued operation of North Carolina Central University School of Law. His Abbeville County School District v. State of South Carolina opinion as Chief Justice and his sustained advocacy alongside Richard W. Riley against voucher proposals situate the education-funding litigation within a longer trajectory tracing from Brown through the post-2000 funding disparities he calls the Corridor of Shame.

chapters.title: Father, Aunts, and Smithfield Origins
chapters.summary: Finney describes being raised by his school-teacher father and his aunts after his mother died when he was ten days old. He recalls his father as a Virginia State University graduate, a strict but fair head of household, and a teacher who moved frequently between Virginia elementary schools whenever a better job appeared.
related_events: Childhood in Virginia public schools

chapters.title: From the Pentagon to Claflin
chapters.summary: Finney traces his father's career from Virginia teaching to wartime work at the Pentagon, then to Morgan State University in Baltimore, then to a position as Dean of Claflin College in Orangeburg, South Carolina around 1945-46. He arrives in Orangeburg expecting lynchings from his Baltimore friends' warnings, only to find a welcoming community.
related_events: World War II domestic federal employment
Family relocation to South Carolina

chapters.title: The Weber Soda Shop and the Klan
chapters.summary: Finney recalls working six to eight years as a soda jerk at the Weber family's college soda shop on the perimeter of Claflin and South Carolina State. He witnesses a Ku Klux Klan parade down the street in front of the two campuses during his first or second year at Claflin, and identifies the Trinity United Methodist Church across the street as his family's church home.
related_events: Ku Klux Klan parade past Claflin and South Carolina State

chapters.title: Observing Black Lawyers in Washington
chapters.summary: Finney describes how observing Black attorneys in Washington during the family's Baltimore years shaped his ambition to enter law. He names Belford V. Lawson Jr. — a Washington lawyer, fraternity president, and national leader — as a figure he wanted to hitch his star to.
related_events: Observation of Black legal practice in DC

chapters.title: South Carolina State Law School
chapters.summary: Finney enters South Carolina State University School of Law in fall 1952, describing small classes of about four students with dedicated faculty. He names John H. Wrighten III (likely) or John W. Wrighten as the student whose lawsuit helped desegregate the University of South Carolina, and recalls graduating and taking the bar in early summer 1954, weeks after the Brown decision. He laments South Carolina's eventual closure of the law school, contrasting it with North Carolina Central University School of Law's continued operation.
keywords: South Carolina State University School of Law
North Carolina Central University School of Law
related_events: Brown v. Board of Education decision
Desegregation of the University of South Carolina

chapters.title: Conway, the Ocean Forest, and Early Practice
chapters.summary: Finney describes teaching school in Conway, South Carolina, while picking up work at the Ocean Forest Hotel as a waiter. He recounts attending his first South Carolina Bar Association meeting in 1954 or 1955 as a waiter — Black bar members were excluded from the social events — and his later return to the same body's head table as Chief Justice.
related_events: 1954/55 South Carolina Bar Association annual meeting

chapters.title: Move to Sumter and Civil Rights Practice
chapters.summary: Finney describes how a mission encountering Matthew J. Perry Jr. and I. DeQuincey Newman around the opening of a state park stimulated his decision to leave Conway for Sumter, South Carolina and become a full-time civil rights attorney. The President of Morris College offers him a part-time South Carolina history teaching position that eases the transition. He identifies I. DeQuincey Newman as field secretary of the SC NAACP.
related_events: State park desegregation litigation
Transition to full-time civil rights practice

chapters.title: The Friendship Nine and Jail-Without-Bail
chapters.summary: Finney recounts representing the Friendship Nine, students from Friendship Junior College in Rock Hill, South Carolina, who were connected with the Congress of Racial Equality through field secretary James T. McCain of Sumter. After his ragged car broke down on the way to court, he reached Rock Hill to learn after the convictions that the students had adopted a new jail-without-bail strategy he had not been informed of. He notes the presence of SNCC, the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, in Rock Hill at the same time.
related_events: Friendship Nine sit-in arrests in Rock Hill
Jail-without-bail tactic adoption

chapters.title: NAACP, Civil Rights Commission, Orangeburg Massacre
chapters.summary: Finney describes his closest affiliation with the Sumter NAACP chapter and the cadre of Black attorneys, mostly South Carolina State law graduates, who took non-lucrative civil rights cases. He details joining the South Carolina Advisory Committee to the US Commission on Civil Rights in 1963 — naming James McBride Dabbs as an active white participant — and resigning along with Ben Payton in 1969 after the federal government refused to reinvestigate the 1968 Orangeburg Massacre.
related_events: 1968 Orangeburg Massacre
1969 resignation from US Commission on Civil Rights

chapters.title: State House, Black Caucus, RFK Job Offer
chapters.summary: After three losing campaigns, Finney wins a seat in the South Carolina House of Representatives in 1972 and helps found the Legislative Black Caucus, prioritizing learning the chamber's rules. He recalls drawing 20-25 percent of the white vote in his Sumter elections and recounts an earlier offer from Robert F. Kennedy to join the US Justice Department, which he declined to remain in South Carolina politics. He also recalls running unsuccessfully for Congress, possibly against Albert W. Watson.
related_events: 1972 SC House election
Founding of the SC Legislative Black Caucus
Robert F. Kennedy DOJ job offer

chapters.title: Supreme Court, Abbeville, and the Death Penalty
chapters.summary: Finney describes his 1985 election to the South Carolina Supreme Court — running against a senator while he himself worked the 124-member House — and his 1994 elevation to Chief Justice. He explains the Abbeville County School District v. State of South Carolina decision and the structural difficulty of getting tax-base counties to share with poorer counties in the Corridor of Shame. He closes by recounting the death-penalty case as a circuit judge — a defendant who robbed a coin dealer and raped two women in Horry County, one of whom died — and the inner struggle of sentencing a human being to die despite his legislative-era opposition to capital punishment.
related_events: 1985 SC Supreme Court election
1994 Chief Justice election
Abbeville County School District v. State of South Carolina decision
